agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation is a critical lifecycle event in logistics and courier operations. A user-initiated cancellation must respect the physical reality of fulfillment — once a package is in transit, cancellation has fundamentally different cost and operational implications than cancelling a newly-placed order. The business must balance customer flexibility with operational cost recovery.","bounded_context":"Order Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A request by a user for pickup and delivery of goods, tracked through a defined lifecycle from placement to completion."},{"term":"Order Status","definition":"The current lifecycle state of an order (e.g., Placed, Confirmed, Dispatched, In Transit, Delivered, Cancelled)."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"A user-initiated request to terminate an order before it reaches a terminal state (Delivered or already Cancelled)."},{"term":"Cancellation Window","definition":"The period during which an order is eligible for cancellation, determined by its current status in the fulfillment lifecycle."},{"term":"Refund","definition":"The return of payment to the user upon successful cancellation, which may be full or partial depending on fulfillment progress."},{"term":"Terminal State","definition":"An order status from which no further transitions are permitted (Delivered, Cancelled)."},{"term":"Dispatched","definition":"A courier has been assigned and is en route to pick up the order. Physical resources are now committed."},{"term":"In Transit","definition":"The courier has possession of the goods and is en route to the delivery destination."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may only be cancelled if it is NOT in a terminal state (Delivered or Cancelled).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Allowing cancellation of a delivered order corrupts order history and breaks financial reconciliation. Cancelling an already-cancelled order is a logical impossibility. These are state integrity constraints.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with status Delivered or Cancelled, when the user requests cancellation, then the system MUST reject the request."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Only the user who placed the order may initiate a cancellation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Allowing arbitrary users to cancel other users' orders is a data integrity and authorization violation. This is a security invariant, not a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"Given a cancellation request where the requesting user ID does not match the order's owning user ID, the system MUST reject the request with an authorization error."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"When an order is cancelled, its status transitions to Cancelled and no further status transitions are permitted.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Cancelled is a terminal state. If a cancelled order could transition back to active, it would corrupt fulfillment tracking, billing, and courier assignments.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been cancelled, when any status transition is attempted, then the system MUST reject it."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A full refund is issued when the order is cancelled before dispatch. A partial refund (or no refund) may apply once the order has been dispatched or is in transit.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Refund policy is a business decision that leadership can adjust. The thresholds and percentages are policy, not physics.","testable_assertion":"Given an order cancelled with status Placed or Confirmed, then refund amount equals 100% of the order total. Given an order cancelled with status Dispatched or In Transit, then refund amount follows the configured partial-refund policy."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"When a dispatched or in-transit order is cancelled, the assigned courier must be notified immediately.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Notification timing and channel are operational policy. The business could decide to batch notifications or change the notification window.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an assigned courier is cancelled, then a cancellation notification event is emitted targeting that courier within the configured SLA."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A cancellation reason must be recorded with every cancellation for operational analytics and dispute resolution.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Whether a reason is mandatory or optional is a product policy decision. However, for auditability it is currently required.","testable_assertion":"Given a cancellation request with no reason provided, then the system MUST reject the request with a validation error."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"All cancellation events must be persisted in an immutable audit log with timestamp, actor, previous status, and reason.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Audit integrity is a hard constraint for financial reconciliation and dispute resolution. Without an immutable record, the system cannot prove what happened.","testable_assertion":"Given any successful cancellation, then an audit log entry exists containing order ID, user ID, timestamp, previous order status, and cancellation reason."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order exists with status Placed or Confirmed","when":"The owning user requests cancellation with a valid reason","then":"The order status transitions to Cancelled, a full refund is initiated, and an audit log entry is created."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order exists with status Dispatched","when":"The owning user requests cancellation with a valid reason","then":"The order status transitions to Cancelled, the assigned courier is notified, a partial refund is initiated per policy, and an audit log entry is created."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order exists with status In Transit","when":"The owning user requests cancellation with a valid reason","then":"The order status transitions to Cancelled, the assigned courier is notified, a refund is calculated per the in-transit cancellation policy, and an audit log entry is created."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order exists with status Delivered","when":"The owning user requests cancellation","then":"The system rejects the request with a clear error message stating the order has already been delivered and cannot be cancelled."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"An order exists with status Cancelled","when":"The owning user requests cancellation again","then":"The system rejects the request with a clear error message stating the order is already cancelled (idempotency — no side effects, no duplicate refund)."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An order exists owned by User A","when":"User B requests cancellation of that order","then":"The system rejects the request with an authorization error. No status change occurs. No audit log entry for a successful cancellation is created."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An order exists in a cancellable state","when":"The owning user requests cancellation without providing a reason","then":"The system rejects the request with a validation error indicating a cancellation reason is required."},{"id":"AC-8","given":"An order is cancelled successfully","when":"Any subsequent status transition is attempted (e.g., mark as Dispatched)","then":"The system rejects the transition, preserving the terminal Cancelled state."},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A cancellation request is submitted","when":"The refund service is temporarily unavailable","then":"The cancellation must NOT be committed. The system returns an error and the order remains in its original state. No partial state corruption occurs."},{"id":"AC-10","given":"Two concurrent cancellation requests arrive for the same order","when":"Both are processed simultaneously","then":"Exactly one succeeds and one fails gracefully. The order is cancelled exactly once, one refund is issued, and one audit entry is created."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"User cancels an order at the exact moment the courier marks it as Dispatched (race condition on status transition).","expected_behavior":"Exactly one transition wins. If cancellation wins, courier is notified the order no longer exists. If dispatch wins, the user sees the order is now Dispatched and may still cancel under the dispatched-cancellation policy. No inconsistent state is permitted."},{"scenario":"Refund service is down when cancellation is requested.","expected_behavior":"The cancellation is NOT partially applied. The system must not transition the order to Cancelled without guaranteeing the refund will be processed. Either use a saga/compensation pattern or reject the cancellation with a retryable error."},{"scenario":"User submits duplicate cancellation requests in rapid succession.","expected_behavior":"The second request is treated idempotently — no duplicate refund, no duplicate audit entry, no error surfaced to user (or a benign 'already cancelled' response)."},{"scenario":"Order has multiple items and only some have been picked up by the courier.","expected_behavior":"If partial fulfillment is not supported, the entire order is cancelled and all items are handled. If partial cancellation is supported, this must be explicitly scoped — but the PM spec says 'cancel their order' (singular, whole-order), so partial cancellation is out of scope unless clarified."},{"scenario":"User cancels and immediately places a new identical order.","expected_behavior":"The new order is treated as a completely independent entity. No state leakage from the cancelled order. The cancelled order's refund proceeds independently."},{"scenario":"Cancellation is requested for an order with an active payment dispute or chargeback.","expected_behavior":"The system must check for existing financial holds or disputes. Cancellation should either be blocked pending dispute resolution or coordinated with the payment provider to avoid double-refund."},{"scenario":"Network timeout — user clicks cancel, request times out, user retries.","expected_behavior":"The system handles this idempotently. If the first request succeeded, the retry sees 'already cancelled.' If the first request never arrived, the retry processes normally."},{"scenario":"Order is in a transitional or processing state (e.g., payment pending, awaiting courier assignment).","expected_behavior":"The system must define whether intermediate/transient states are cancellable. At minimum, any state before Dispatched should allow cancellation. Transient states must not block cancellation indefinitely."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Cancellation directly affects revenue recognition. Full refunds on pre-dispatch orders are low cost. Post-dispatch cancellations incur sunk courier costs (fuel, time, opportunity cost). A missing or broken refund flow creates chargeback risk and regulatory exposure. Duplicate refunds due to race conditions cause direct financial loss.","operational":"Courier notification latency on post-dispatch cancellations creates wasted trips, increasing operational cost and reducing courier utilization. High cancellation rates may signal upstream UX or pricing problems. Audit log integrity is essential for dispute resolution workflows.","user":"Cancellation is a trust-building feature — users who know they can cancel are more likely to place orders. Poor cancellation UX (confusing errors, slow refunds, inability to cancel) erodes trust and increases support ticket volume. Clear feedback on cancellation eligibility by status is critical.","risk":"Primary risks: (1) Race conditions causing inconsistent state between order status and payment, (2) Duplicate refunds from non-idempotent processing, (3) Missing audit trail creating legal/compliance exposure, (4) Post-dispatch cancellations without courier notification causing phantom deliveries.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Users (Senders)","impact":"Gain ability to cancel orders with clear refund expectations. Trust and satisfaction increase. Poor implementation causes frustration and support escalation."},{"group":"Couriers","impact":"Must receive timely cancellation notifications to avoid wasted trips. Late notification directly wastes their time and erodes courier satisfaction and retention."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Refund processing must reconcile cleanly. Audit logs are required for financial reporting. Duplicate or missing refunds create reconciliation nightmares."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Must have visibility into cancellation status, reason, and refund state to handle disputes. Clear audit trail reduces resolution time."},{"group":"Product / Analytics","impact":"Cancellation reasons provide signal on product issues, pricing sensitivity, and competitor dynamics. Missing reasons degrade this insight."}]},"confidence":{"level":55,"reasoning":"The PM spec is a single sentence — 'Users should be able to cancel their order.' This lacks specificity on refund policy details, which order statuses exist in this system, whether partial cancellation is in scope, cancellation SLAs, and the payment/refund integration model. The business rules and acceptance criteria above are grounded in logistics domain conventions, but several details require confirmation from the PM and the existing codebase. Confidence is capped due to ambiguity.","high_confidence_areas":["Terminal states (Delivered, Cancelled) must block cancellation — this is a universal invariant","Only the order owner may cancel — authorization invariant","Audit logging is required for any state-changing financial operation","Courier notification is required for post-dispatch cancellations"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact refund percentages for post-dispatch cancellation","Whether partial order cancellation is in scope","Specific order statuses in this system's lifecycle model","Payment provider integration and saga/compensation patterns","Whether cancellation reason is mandatory or optional in this product's UX"],"assumptions":["The order lifecycle includes at minimum: Placed, Confirmed, Dispatched, In Transit, Delivered, Cancelled","Refund policy follows a full-before-dispatch, partial-after-dispatch model","Cancellation is whole-order only (not partial/line-item)","Cancellation reason is mandatory","The system has an assigned courier model for dispatch"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The PM spec does not define which order statuses exist or which are cancellable. The acceptance criteria assume a standard logistics lifecycle (Placed → Confirmed → Dispatched → In Transit → Delivered) but the actual system may differ.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product Management"],"options":["Confirm the order lifecycle states with the PM and codebase","Assume standard logistics lifecycle and document the assumption"],"recommendation":"Confirm the actual order status enum from the codebase before implementation. Do not assume."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Refund policy for post-dispatch cancellation is undefined. Is it a fixed percentage? Sliding scale based on courier proximity? No refund at all? This has direct financial impact.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","End Users","Product Management"],"options":["Full refund regardless of status","No refund after dispatch","Partial refund with configurable policy per status"],"recommendation":"Define explicit refund rules per order status with Finance and PM sign-off before implementation."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information on whether this system supports partial order cancellation (cancelling individual items within a multi-item order). The PM said 'cancel their order' which implies whole-order, but multi-item orders may need partial cancellation.","affected_stakeholders":["End Users","Engineering","Product Management"],"options":["Whole-order cancellation only (MVP)","Support partial cancellation from the start"],"recommendation":"Ship whole-order cancellation first. Partial cancellation is a separate feature with significantly more complexity (item-level refunds, partial courier trips)."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation is a critical lifecycle event in logistics and courier operations. A user-initiated cancellation must respect the physical reality of fulfillment — once a package is in transit, cancellation has fundamentally different cost and operational implications than cancelling a newly-placed order. The business must balance customer flexibility with operational cost recovery.","bounded_context":"Order Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"O
